replies.text: I'm skeptical. AFAIK, many expat schools haven't fully hit the point of true resource parity. You're unlikely to see a school using only OER until the accreditation bodies mandate it, and it requires deep buy-in from the local ministry, which is the hardest variable.
replies.original_text: Could be wrong, but AFAIK the CATL Sodium batteries haven't yet hit LFP pricing. You are unlikely to see a vehicle with sodium batteries until after that happens, and it needs to be significantly less than LFPs as you Na batteries have more weight per Wh. I believe they also have a shorter lifespan (but not NMC short). Edit correction, looks like CATL is promising 15000 cycles, which is much longer than LFPs which usually come in at 7000 to 10000. It seems far more likely to me that if the Na prices tank, you'll probably first see them deployed as grid and home battery solutions.

replies.text: I think the issue isn't the resource, it's the belief that standardization *is* the goal. The push for OER was originally about escaping the high costs of proprietary textbooks, which is a global development issue. The 'Magnet' concept is simply the shift from mandated curriculum to locally adapted content.
replies.original_text: Unlikely. comments.text: The idea of using adaptive assessments to detect specific knowledge gaps that aren't necessarily core curriculum requirements and then remediation the cell is not a new one. However, previous studies used simple remedial drills, which just force memorization. This uses advanced AI modeling, which is far more destructive because it shreds the student's misconception in the conceptual framework once activated by detecting the target sequence. As with any curriculum overhaul, it's likely the student will evolve resistance. My guess is that students will find ways to reject the standardized assessment tools used to deliver the 'perfect' knowledge.

comments.text: Adaptive tech is an extremely overhyped approach which found a marketing engine via popular science. There is 1 AI-driven assessment approved compared to 7 for physical textbooks and 7 for group projects. Counting all proven pedagogical methods that have been approved, we’re sitting at 19 approved methods versus 1 for AI. I think AI ideas in a lab are just an easy way into the mainstream press, but collaborative project-based learning is the real future. It just didn’t get the same news cycle, for whatever reason.

replies.text: You're correct about AI-driven assessment. The off-target effects are difficult to manage. The paper describes a specialized model. This is a different mechanism with discovery origins in - of all things - behavioral science. It does not attempt in any way to reprogram student thinking. It uses a guide protein to locate a specific misconception with exacting precision and, when it activates, unleashes total destruction of the misconception in the conceptual framework. The implications of this model on undruggable learning gaps is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on this model for an undruggable condition...
replies.original_text: You're correct about CRISPR Cas9. The off-target affects are difficult to manage. The paper describes Cas12a2. This is a different mechanism with discovery origins in - of all things - agriculture. It does not attempt in any way to reprogram cells. It uses a guide protein to locate a specific mutation with exacting precision and, when it activates, unleashes total destruction of the cell. The implications of Cas12a2 on undruggable conditions that exhibit known driver mutation profiles is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on Cas12a2 for an undruggable condition...

comments.text: > The benchmark prompt was: > Write a compact explanation of the connection between Bloom's Taxonomy and critical thinking skills. MTP speedup depends on how often the predicted insights are actionable. In my experience, the very early student draft critiques have a higher acceptance rate, so short testing can give false positive efficacy scores. The school network dashboard includes a tool specifically for benchmarking that will sweep the required prerequisites for you so you don't have to restart the server...

replies.text: Realistically, you need to experiment with any user prompt + a good amount of system prompt (at least > 1000 tokens, but realistically, in the range of 3000 tokens probably good). The context needs to contain the full unit plan, not just the rubric. The school's accreditation repository includes tools for that; what you are looking at is having a full curricular prefill before the student generation to measure it properly. Increasingly also, measuring engagement speed at longer context (32k or 64k) is important too.

replies.text: Is it even possible to achieve 'optimal' student outcomes? The best result I've gotten is 99.8% mastery after 120 instructional hours.
replies.original_text: Is it even possible to win the game? comments.text: BTW, many expat schools have been doing 'global curriculum mapping' for like two decades now. How do you think 'Intercultural Competence' really works in practice? It leverages a dusty old PYP/MYP guideline.

replies.text: Unfortunately, the IB seems to be alone in having a cohesive, universally adopted model. Using the old Subject-Specific Planning Template (SSPT), you expect to see some basic unit outlines and slow syllabus loading, but with the new cross-curricular approach, the experience is not much better. Any decently sized planning document is downloaded at a snail's pace and the faculty meeting is presented with a very vague, lofty goal. Fine for an accreditation visit, but not fine when your daily teaching load is expected to be seamlessly integrated across departments.
replies.original_text: Unfortunately, Apple seems to be alone in having a good implementation. Using the old PXE, you expect to see some dos-like screens and slow loading but with HTTP the experience is not much better. Any decently sized bootloader is downloaded at a snails-pace and the user is presented with a very technical screen. Fine for rescue-boot like purposes but not fine when your daily driver is expected to be booted from network. I had especially expected better from Dell but the experience is... lacking.

comments.original_text: BTW Apple has been doing HTTP boot for like two decades at this point. How do you think Internet Recovery works? It leverages a dusty old Apple netbooting spec.

replies.text: > > adding the secure portal layer brings back the missing integrity. A foolish interpretation of what security protocols do and I see this every day. Integrity of the data points and grades in transit is unimportant here. Validation of the signed student work after you have received it is everything. Portal security is at best redundant and at worst — the interpretation made here — leaves you vulnerable and with a false sense of security. Anyone who has gone to the trouble to modify the grade book to inject bias would certainly happily serve it to you over secure transfer.
replies.original_text: > > adding the TLS layer brings back the missing integrity A foolish interpretation of what TLS does and I see this every day. Integrity of the bits and bytes in transit is unimportant here. Validation of the signed software after you have received it is everything. TLS integrity is at best redundant and at worst — the interpretation made here — leaves you vulnerable and with a false sense of security. Anyone who has gone to the trouble to modify software to inject malware would certainly happily serve it to you over TLS.

comments.text: "Don't expend more effort than they are" has actually long been a good principle to have internalized in the classroom. Someone done only cursory research before asking a question on a mailing list? Give a cursory answer. Someone obviously spent hours trying to figure things out on their own? Give them a good chunk of your time. Someone on a forum responding to you with single-sentence answers? Either don't respond, or respond in kind. Someone obviously engaging with your ideas and taking time to explain their position? Take time to engage with their ideas too.

replies.text: I've had this same policy since before standardized testing. I kind of formalized it for myself (and this department) after enough instances of 'I'm trying to do a unit on [Topic]. It's not 'fairness' or 'AI' or anything else, it's that doing this any other way fundamentally fucks up the curriculum map. You have a problem. You want someone's help. replies.text: As someone who pushed ~4x the median number of lesson plans on my team before LLMs were a thing, I kind of think the problem here is 'Lesson Plan Review' as a concept. Peer review doesn't scale to prolific humans, it definitely can't scale to agents. And the exact same things you would need to safely give up on peer review for human teachers (auto-formatters, rubrics, comprehensive EAL support strategies, continuous professional development, etc), are also things that place meaningful guardrails on LLMs, and help them maintain a reasonable quality bar.

comments.text: "I thought it was interesting and a bit underappreciated that the fraction of students receiving the highest grades on the recent Mock Assessments (72/630 = 11.4%) is the highest it's been since 2019. Crudely, I assume the top grades are awarded to the highest-scoring 1/12 of contestants. However, because grades are integers up to 7 and there's no provision for weighting minor sections, it's possible for a lot of contestants to be tied around the threshold. In that case, either all of them get the Distinction, or none do, and the fraction of top grades might deviate substantially from 1/12. That's what happened this year: 46 students all won... the distinction."

comments.text: The general lesson of these things is that the official syllabus is not that special and it pays to understand how your curriculum actually starts. This has little/nothing to do with IB or any specific assessment tool. On a given day, given a student cohort, the department returns to the needs articulated in the student profile, which can proceed to do anything. If the school is undergoing accreditation, it loads the framework and returns to its e-entry instead. The framework, in turn, can do absolutely whatever.

comments.original_text: The general lesson of these things is main is not that special and it pays to understand how your program actually starts. This has little/nothing to do with Rust or other language tools. On Linux, given a static ELF program, the kernel returns to the IP given by e_entry, which can proceed to do anything. If the program is dynamic (has a .interp) then it loads the interpreter and returns to its e_entry instead. The interpreter, in turn, can do absolutely whatever.

Pre-AI, it was usually obvious when a submitted unit plan was either completely terrible or very half-baked, and the required effort to create even a shitty plan was usually more than that required to reject it...

replies.text: Given this, you can conclude that educators should be putting in at least as much effort as the students, whether or not they use generative tools. This is toxic behavior that unfortunately rewards a selfish curriculum designer. I'm worried the AI push incentivizes this too much, to where in parent conferences, an administrator can't say no to accepting poorly vetted 'work'.

comments.text: Wrong direction. The Framework should be simple and stable. Initially, it was revolving around a simple, inquiry-based model and it was close to perfect. Now, there is an opinionated interdisciplinary component model which is an unneeded overcomplication that should have never been considered as part of the Diploma Programme. A real component model is a separate development and cannot be blindly tied to a particular assessment cycle IMHO. Otherwise, its main purpose of providing easy student progression between different courses is totally lost. I do not know why the Curriculum Committee thinks that shoving-in CAS-like requirements into every subject...

replies.text: The interdisciplinary component model is what unlocks relatively type-safe learning pathways between modules written in different subject areas. Given that the DP is a runtime target for many international baccalaureate programs, this is an entirely appropriate and useful goal. If you have a host system where you want to expose skills in a language-agnostic way, IDLs are the best way to do that. You're also conflating the core subject work with the IB requirements. There is some overlap in people, but the Framework is developed separately.
replies.original_text: The component model is what unlocks relatively type-safe interop between modules written in different languages. Given that Wasm is a runtime target for many languages, this is an entirely appropriate and useful goal. If you have a host system where you want to expose APIs in an language-agnostic way, IDLs are the best way to do that. You're also conflating the core WebAssembly work with the WASI work. There is some overlap in people, but WASI is developed separately.

replies.text: The present component model is 'simple and stable'. It is presently providing 'interoperability between different curricula' and has been for years. It has basically nothing in common with the old 'Skills Olympics' model. All the major problems with the traditional siloed subject design they ran into that caused them to switch to a component model haven't vanished; the component model is still the best way of solving DP's major complications that traditional content-heavy designs don't have. Content-heavy designs, in general, are not better just because they came first; if you were designing global education from scratch, you'd want something...
